claims.claim: RM18 billion highway concession terms remain classified for 30 years
claims.source: Ministry of Finance Malaysia press statement, 26 June 2019: https://www.mof.gov.my/portal/arkib/media/2019/jun/sm260719_Tawaran_Pengambilalihan_4_Lebuhraya.pdf; Parliament Hansard, Dewan Rakyat, 21 March 2024: https://repositori.parlimen.gov.my/bitstream/123456789/3427/17/DR16-21032024.pdf
claims.correction: The RM18 billion figure is verified as an estimated compensation cost if toll collection were abolished for four highways, not the value of classified concession terms. Parliament verified that the PJD Link concession agreement was classified under OSA, but this does not prove all RM18 billion of highway concession terms are classified for 30 years.
claims.stale_check: No evidence that a recent change makes the claim accurate as of 17 May 2026.

claims.claim: Concession agreements for major toll highways worth RM18 billion remain classified under OSA
claims.source: Ministry of Finance Malaysia press statement, 26 June 2019: https://www.mof.gov.my/portal/arkib/media/2019/jun/sm260719_Tawaran_Pengambilalihan_4_Lebuhraya.pdf; Parliament Hansard, Dewan Rakyat, 21 March 2024: https://repositori.parlimen.gov.my/bitstream/123456789/3427/17/DR16-21032024.pdf
claims.correction: Use narrower wording: at least one highway concession agreement, PJD Link, was described in Parliament in March 2024 as classified under OSA and subject to confidentiality clauses. The RM18 billion figure should not be attached to classified contract value; it referred to compensation if toll collection stopped.

claims.claim: Full terms not scheduled for disclosure until 2035 at the earliest
claims.correction: I found no primary source establishing a scheduled disclosure date of 2035. Parliament instead said PJD Link would need to be declassified before contents could be disclosed and that confidentiality clauses also applied.
claims.stale_check: No evidence of a recent disclosure schedule change found.

claims.claim: Malaysians pay tolls under contracts they are legally forbidden from reading
claims.source: Parliament Hansard, Dewan Rakyat, 21 March 2024: https://repositori.parlimen.gov.my/bitstream/123456789/3427/17/DR16-21032024.pdf; Lembaga Lebuhraya Malaysia toll division page, updated 17 April 2026: https://www.llm.gov.my/corporate_info/dept_toll
claims.correction: The public does pay tolls and Parliament confirms at least PJD Link was classified under OSA, but the blanket statement about all toll contracts being legally forbidden to read is not verified.

claims.claim: OSA Section 2A classifies concession agreements as government confidential documents
claims.source: Official Secrets Act 1972, Act 88, Attorney General's Chambers Malaysia: https://www.agc.gov.my/agcportal/uploads/files/Publications/LOM/EN/Act%2088.pdf
claims.correction: Section 2A concerns addition, deletion, or amendment of the Schedule. Classification of official documents is under Section 2B, and the definition of official secret is in Section 2. Concession agreements are not automatically classified by Section 2A.
claims.stale_check: No relevant recent amendment found in the checked sources.

claims.claim: Disclosing toll road terms carries criminal penalties including imprisonment
claims.source: Official Secrets Act 1972, Act 88, Attorney General's Chambers Malaysia: https://www.agc.gov.my/agcportal/uploads/files/Publications/LOM/EN/Act%2088.pdf; Parliament Hansard, Dewan Rakyat, 21 March 2024: https://repositori.parlimen.gov.my/bitstream/123456789/3427/17/DR16-21032024.pdf
claims.correction: If the terms are classified as official secrets, wrongful communication can carry imprisonment under OSA Section 8. The claim should say 'classified toll road terms', not toll road terms generally.

claims.claim: Toll collection exceeded RM7.2 billion in 2025 paid entirely by Malaysian motorists
claims.source: Lembaga Lebuhraya Malaysia toll division page, updated 17 April 2026: https://www.llm.gov.my/corporate_info/dept_toll; LLM annual reports page, updated 14 May 2026: https://www.llm.gov.my/publication/yearbook
claims.correction: I found official confirmation that LLM verifies traffic and toll revenue, but not a primary source for RM7.2 billion in 2025. The 'paid entirely by Malaysian motorists' wording is also overbroad because toll payers can include commercial vehicles, foreign motorists, and other vehicle classes.
claims.stale_check: The 2025 annual figure may not yet be available in an official annual report; no primary 2025 total was found.

claims.claim: Consumers bear full cost but have zero visibility into profit-sharing arrangements
claims.source: Lembaga Lebuhraya Malaysia toll division page, updated 17 April 2026: https://www.llm.gov.my/corporate_info/dept_toll; Parliament Hansard, Dewan Rakyat, 21 March 2024: https://repositori.parlimen.gov.my/bitstream/123456789/3427/17/DR16-21032024.pdf
claims.correction: LLM states it verifies toll revenue, compensation, toll revenue sharing, and related certificates, but public access to concession terms is constrained for at least PJD Link. 'Full cost' and 'zero visibility' are overclaims unless supported by contract-specific evidence.

claims.claim: Indonesia publishes all highway concession terms on public procurement portal
claims.correction: Indonesia's BPJT says toll road concession agreement documents are excepted from public access under Indonesia's public information law because they contain business and contractual confidential information.
claims.stale_check: This is a 2025 official BPJT FAQ and remains the most recent primary source found.

claims.claim: Regional neighbour proves infrastructure concession transparency is achievable
claims.correction: The Indonesia comparison is not supported for highway concession agreements; BPJT states such agreements are excluded from public access.

claims.claim: The public pays through tolls but cannot evaluate whether the terms are fair until 2035
claims.correction: The public pays tolls, and some agreement terms are not publicly accessible, but I found no primary source proving that evaluation must wait until 2035.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: RM18 billion highway concession terms remain classified for 30 years
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The RM18 billion figure was compensation if tolls were abolished for four highways, not the value of classified concession terms.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: full terms not scheduled for disclosure until 2035 at the earliest
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No primary source found for a 2035 disclosure schedule.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: OSA Section 2A classifies concession agreements as government confidential documents
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Section 2A is about amending the Schedule; classification is under other OSA provisions, especially Section 2B.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Toll collection exceeded RM7.2 billion in 2025
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No primary source was found for this 2025 toll collection figure.
The wording excludes commercial vehicles, foreign users, and other toll-paying categories without evidence.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Indonesia publishes all highway concession terms on public procurement portal
cardinal_sin_flags.why: BPJT states toll road concession agreement documents are excluded from public access.

omitted_facts: Malaysia's OSA Section 2B, not Section 2A, is the operative provision for appointing public officers to classify official documents, information, or material: https://www.agc.gov.my/agcportal/uploads/files/Publications/LOM/EN/Act%2088.pdf

Parliament recorded in March 2024 that PJD Link's concession agreement would need to be declassified before contents could be openly disclosed, and that contractual confidentiality clauses also applied: https://repositori.parlimen.gov.my/bitstream/123456789/3427/17/DR16-21032024.pdf

LLM's Toll Surveillance Division states it verifies traffic and toll revenue, toll compensation, toll revenue sharing, and related certificates, which complicates the claim of 'zero visibility' without specifying public visibility: https://www.llm.gov.my/corporate_info/dept_toll

Indonesia's BPJT explicitly says toll road concession agreement documents are excluded from public access under the public information framework because they contain business and contractual confidential information: https://bpjt.pu.go.id/faq/apakah-dokumen-perjanjian-pengusahaan-jalan-tol-dapat-diakses-publik/
source_assessment: The original analysis cites zero sources. It does not cite Malaysian legal text, Parliament, LLM, MOF, concessionaire filings, court rulings, Indonesian BPJT materials, or public information law sources. The institutional range is therefore absent: no government primary sources, no regulator sources, no concessionaire perspective, no civil society source, no legal expert, and no affected-user evidence. Source diversity is effectively an echo chamber because readers are given no source trail at all.
overall_assessment: The analysis is factually unreliable. Its core legal claim cites the wrong OSA section, its RM18 billion figure is used in the wrong context, its 2035 disclosure timeline is unverified, its 2025 toll collection figure is unverified, and its Indonesia comparison is contradicted by BPJT's own public FAQ. A narrower, supportable version would say that at least one highway concession agreement, PJD Link, has been described in Parliament as OSA-classified and subject to confidentiality clauses, but the published cards go well beyond what the primary sources support.
